target.title: fix(feishu): parse all merge-forward sub-messages and extract interactive card text
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Feishu merge_forward messages containing interactive/card sub-messages (tables, dashboards, report cards) were silently dropped or rendered as bare `[interactive]`. Two bugs: (1) `parseMergeForwardContent` only included sub-messages with `upper_message_id`, but interactive cards may lack this field; (2) `formatSubMessageContent` had no case for `"interactive"` and fell through to the generic `[contentType]` format.

**Solution**: (1) Broaden the sub-message filter to include all items except the root merge_forward container; (2) Add an `"interactive"` case that reuses `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` — the same parser already used in production for fetched message card rendering.

**What changed**: 
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot-content.ts`: +14/-2 — filter fix + interactive card parsing via shared `parseInteractiveCardContent`
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts`: +122 — 2 new regression tests (unit + integration with mocked Feishu API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts`: +1/-1 — export `parseInteractiveCardContent`

**What did NOT change**:
- The `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser itself — unchanged, just exported
- Other sub-message type handling (text, image, sticker, etc.) — unchanged
- The Feishu API fetch path for merge_forward detail — unchanged

Fixes #77909

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu merge_forward messages containing interactive/card sub-messages were dropped or rendered as bare `[interactive]` placeholder text. Card header titles, table data, and body content were lost before reaching the agent.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` rebased onto `upstream/main` (`8c09419f2`)
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Vitest**: 4.1.8

**Root cause — two independent bugs in the same code path**:

Bug 1 (filter): `parseMergeForwardContent` at `bot-content.ts:230` originally used:
```typescript
const subMessages = items.filter((item) => item.upper_message_id);
```
Interactive/card items returned by the Feishu merge_forward detail API may not include `upper_message_id`. These items were filtered out before reaching `formatSubMessageContent`, so their content was completely lost.

Bug 2 (format): `formatSubMessageContent` in `bot-content.ts:198` had no case for `contentType === "interactive"`, falling through to `default: return `[${contentType}]``. Even if interactive items passed the filter, they'd render as `[interactive]` with no extracted text.

**Detailed fix trace** (specific code changes in `bot-content.ts`):

Filter fix (`bot-content.ts:235-238`):
```typescript
const subMessages = items.filter(
  (item) => Boolean(item.upper_message_id) || item.msg_type !== "merge_forward",
);
```
Now includes items with `upper_message_id` (original behavior) OR items that are NOT root merge_forward containers (new: catches interactive cards, images, text without parent ref).

Format fix (`bot-content.ts:201-203`):
```typescript
case "interactive":
  return parseInteractiveCardContent(parsed);
```
Reuses the production-grade parser that handles template variables (`{variable}`), i18n element tags (`{{i18n key}}`), body.elements (div, markdown, lark_md), and post content fallback. This is the same parser used by `getMessageFeishu` for regular fetched messages.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts` on the rebased `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` branch at Node 24.13 Linux x86_64

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  89 passed (89)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 23.79s
```

Two new regression tests added:

1. **Unit test**: "includes interactive sub-messages without upper_message_id (regression #77909)" — directly calls `parseMergeForwardContent` with a card containing header "Weekly Report" and Sales/Cost body elements. Verifies card text is extracted when `upper_message_id` is absent.

2. **Integration test**: "expands merge_forward interactive card sub-messages (regression #77909)" — via `dispatchMessage` with mocked Feishu API returning an interactive card "Q2 Sales Dashboard" with Revenue/Profit data. Verifies `BodyForAgent` contains header title, body text, and accompanying text message.

**Observed result after the fix**: Card content like headers ("Q2 Sales Dashboard"), table data ("Revenue: ¥50,000", "Profit: ¥12,300"), and markdown body text now correctly reach the agent's `BodyForAgent`. The shared `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser ensures consistent rendering between merge_forward interactive cards and directly-fetched interactive messages. Before: `[interactive]` or silently dropped; after: full card text extraction via shared production parser.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu tenant with real merge_forward messages containing interactive cards. This requires a Feishu app with bot credentials, a group chat with merge_forward messages containing interactive card content, and active WebSocket event delivery. Feishu/Lark tenant access is not available for external contributors — see note below.

**Evidence provenance**: Terminal output from real local checkout of rebased `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` on Node 24.13.1 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-23. The two prior commits (`ea066aeb8` and `74ecd7198`) were squashed into one clean commit, and the CI re-run trigger commit (`e047257a5`) was dropped.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 89/89 | bot.test.ts — all passing including 2 new regression tests |
| Rebase | ✅ | Clean rebase onto upstream/main, 1 squashed commit |
| Scope | ✅ | 3 feishu files only, CI trigger commit removed |

## Design decisions

**Why reuse `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` instead of a simpler local extractor?**

The first implementation (commit `ea066aeb8`, later superseded) used a local `extractInteractiveText()` that only handled basic header/body text traversal. ClawSweeper review correctly identified this as a maintenance risk — two parsers for the same card format would inevitably drift over time as the Feishu card schema evolves or new features are added to the production parser.

The shared parser in `send.ts` handles a comprehensive set of Feishu interactive card features: template variable substitution (`{variable}` → resolved value), i18n element tags (`{{i18n key}}` → translated text via the i18n registry), body.elements iteration (div with text content, markdown blocks, lark_md inline formatting), post content fallback (cards that embed their text in a post-style content field), and config-level text extraction. All of these are potentially relevant to merge_forward interactive cards, which can contain the same card types as directly-fetched messages.

Exporting the function (`function` → `export function`) is a minimal API surface change — the function signature, behavior, and callers are all unchanged. The only difference is that `bot-content.ts` can now import and call it for merge_forward cards. This is the same pattern used by `bot-content.ts`'s existing import of `saveMessageResourceFeishu` from `media.js` — cross-module imports within the same extension are standard practice.

**Why "include all non-root items" instead of "include items with known msg_types"?**

A whitelist of known types would silently drop any future Feishu message types added to merge_forward payloads. The exclusion-based approach ("everything except the root container") is forward-compatible: new message types automatically pass through to `formatSubMessageContent`, which handles unknown types gracefully via the `[${contentType}]` fallback that renders a readable placeholder rather than dropping content entirely.

The root container is excluded because it's always `msg_type: "merge_forward"` and contains only wrapper metadata (merge_forward header/title), not user-visible sub-message content. Including it would produce a duplicate/nested "Merged and Forwarded Message" label. The exclusion uses `item.msg_type !== "merge_forward"` as a negative check — any item whose msg_type IS merge_forward is the container, and any item whose msg_type is anything else (text, image, interactive, sticker, etc.) is a sub-message worth rendering.

**Why can't this be tested with a live Feishu tenant?**

Feishu open platform requires enterprise/organization certification for app creation. Lark international version is more permissive for individual developers but may require network access that is not available from all environments. The OpenClaw QA credential pool (Convex broker) requires write/maintain repository permissions that external contributors do not have. The fix is validated by unit tests using exact Feishu interactive card JSON fixtures matching the documented card message schema, including header.title with plain_text tags, body.elements with div/markdown/lark_md content, and the standard Feishu message envelope structure.

**Why can't this be tested with a live Feishu tenant?**

Feishu open platform requires enterprise/org certification for app creation (personal developers may face restrictions). Lark international version is more permissive but requires network access that may not be available. The OpenClaw QA credential pool (Convex broker) requires write/maintain repository permissions. External contributors cannot access any of these paths. The fix is validated by unit tests using exact Feishu card JSON fixtures matching the documented interactive card schema.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The broadened sub-message filter could theoretically include unexpected item types from the Feishu API that were previously excluded. If the Feishu API returns items with `msg_type` values not handled by `formatSubMessageContent`, they would render as `[unknown_type]` instead of being silently dropped.

**Risk level**: Low risk — `formatSubMessageContent` has a `default: return `[${contentType}]`` fallback for unknown types, so any unexpected sub-message types from the Feishu API produce visible-but-safe placeholder text rather than crashing the handler or leaking raw JSON data into the agent body. The root container exclusion relies on `msg_type !== "merge_forward"` which is the stable API contract for merge_forward wrapper items — this field has been consistent across Feishu API versions.

**Mitigation**: The two new regression tests cover both the filter path (items without `upper_message_id`) and the format path (interactive card content extraction). The integration test mocks the full Feishu API round-trip: `im.message.get` returning merge_forward detail items, `dispatchMessage` processing through the complete handler chain, and `finalizeInboundContext` producing the final agent body. The unit test directly exercises `parseMergeForwardContent` with the exact card JSON fixture from the issue report. The existing 87 bot tests verify zero regressions in other Feishu message handling paths. The `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser is production-proven — it's the same code path used for every directly-fetched Feishu interactive message.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery` — Changes what the agent sees for merge_forward messages. Previously dropped or placeholder content is now rendered as readable text. This is a bug fix restoring correct behavior: the agent should see card content, not `[interactive]`.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. Code changes are mature (one review cycle completed), all 89 feishu tests pass, and the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` gate requires maintainer decision on proof adequacy.

target.title: fix(cron): heal hallucinated everyMs schedule kind and every alias
target.body:
## What

`coerceSchedule` now heals two shapes that models routinely hallucinate when creating recurring cron jobs:

- `kind: "everyMs"` → canonical `kind: "every"`
- interval in an `every` field → folded into `everyMs` (when `everyMs` is absent)

## Why

Recurring jobs are described by `kind: "every"` + `everyMs`. In practice models frequently emit `kind: "everyMs"` and/or put the interval in `every`. Previously:

- `kind: "everyMs"` was not one of `at | every | cron`, so `kind` fell through to `undefined`.
- `every` was ignored entirely, so the interval was dropped.

The result was an invalid schedule that failed validation, so the agent burned turns retrying the same malformed call.

## How

In `coerceSchedule`:

- Map `rawKind === "everyms"` to `"every"` before the kind whitelist check.
- Read the interval from `schedule.everyMs ?? schedule.every`, so the alias flows through the existing strict finite-number coercion (floored, range-checked) — explicit `everyMs` still wins over the alias.
- Drop the non-canonical `every` field from the normalized output.

No behavior change for already-canonical schedules.

## Tests

Added three cases to `src/cron/normalize.test.ts`:

- heals `kind: "everyMs"` → `"every"`
- heals `every` alias → `everyMs` and drops `every`
- explicit `everyMs` wins over `every`

`src/cron/normalize.test.ts` passes (57 tests); typecheck and oxlint clean on the changed files.

target.title: Clear pending tool media delivered via the messaging tool
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users receive a generated image **twice** in chat. In automatic visible-reply mode, a media-producing tool (e.g. `image_generate`) queues its output for automatic source-reply delivery, and when the model *also* sends that same file explicitly via the `message` tool, both deliveries fire. Observed in production as two `sendPhoto` calls for a single `image_generate` completion run.

## Why This Change Was Made

The existing reply-payload dedup (`filterMessagingToolMediaDuplicates`) already filters reply media against `messagingToolSentMediaUrls`, but it compares **normalized full paths** (`normalizeMediaForDedupe` only strips `file://`). The same generated file is referenced by two different prefixes that never normalize to the same string:

- the pending queue holds the **host** path: `/data/media/tool-image-generation/gen-….jpg`
- the `message`-tool args carry the **sandbox-container** path: `/workspace/media/tool-image-generation/gen-….jpg`

So the duplicate slips past the full-path comparison. This change stops it at the source: when messaging-tool media is committed in `handleToolExecutionEnd`, it drops any `pendingToolMediaUrls` entry with the same **basename** (basename matching is what bridges the host/container prefix difference). The drop is gated on a non-error send, so a failed `message`-tool send keeps the pending media as the fallback delivery path. Non-goal: it does not change `filterMessagingToolMediaDuplicates` or other delivery paths.

## User Impact

Users no longer get the same generated image delivered twice when the assistant both auto-attaches tool media and sends it via the `message` tool. No change to single-delivery cases.

## Evidence

Three regression tests added in `embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts`:

- drops queued tool media already delivered via the message tool across host/container paths
- keeps queued tool media the message tool did **not** deliver
- does **not** drop queued media on a failed message-tool send

The first test fails on `main` without the source change and passes with it (the fix is load-bearing):

```
# with the fix:
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)

# source reverted to main, tests kept:
 × drops queued tool media already delivered via the message tool across host/container paths
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  1 failed | 30 passed (31)
```

Root cause additionally confirmed in production: two `sendPhoto` calls logged for one `image_generate` run.

target.title: model-usage: narrow parse_date except Exception to ValueError
target.body:
## What

In `skills/model-usage/scripts/model_usage.py`, `parse_date(value: str)` used `datetime.strptime(value, "%Y-%m-%d").date()` wrapped in a bare `except Exception`.

## Why

`datetime.strptime` with a fixed `'%Y-%m-%d'` format string only raises `ValueError` when the input does not match the format. `TypeError` is not reachable from this function — the function signature is typed `value: str`, and the only caller (`filter_by_days`) already type-checks `entry['date']` with `isinstance(day, str)` before calling.

Narrowing to `except ValueError`:
- Stops the handler from swallowing `KeyboardInterrupt` and `SystemExit` during a slow `strptime` parse (a future call that takes a long time, or someone in the future replaces the simple `strptime` with a heavier parser).
- Stops masking any future bug introduced into the call site (a `NameError` from a typo, an `AttributeError` from a refactor) as `"unparseable date"`. Those should surface as the real exception.

## Tests

Three new tests in `skills/model-usage/scripts/test_model_usage.py`:
- `test_parse_date_accepts_iso_format` — `parse_date("2026-06-19")` returns `date(2026, 6, 19)`.
- `test_parse_date_returns_none_for_malformed_value` — `parse_date("not-a-date")` returns `None`.
- `test_parse_date_returns_none_for_empty_string` — `parse_date("")` returns `None`.

target.title: fix(runtime): protect active work during restarts
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes the core runtime failure mode behind the 2026-06-22 topic UX incident: model/tool-triggered gateway restarts could bypass active-work deferral and interrupt live user-facing work, while delegated tasks could remain too quiet or duplicate-prone.

The incident evidence showed repeated controlled launchd-handoff gateway restarts around 16:24, 16:25, 16:29, 16:54, 17:07, and 17:15 while active work existed, including 4-6 active tasks and 2-3 embedded runs. Restart drain timeout was `0ms`, producing stale resumes, delayed/no finals, duplicate assistant finals, and repeated “are we done?” nudges rather than a crash loop.

## Changes

- Protect active work during safe restart requests by downgrading `skipDeferral` unless `interruptActiveWork` is explicitly approved.
- Add restart audit context with requester/reason and active-work counts.
- Route gateway tool/update restart paths through the safer restart coordination shape.
- Make user-facing delegated/session tasks default to visible `state_changes` while preserving silent/not-applicable handling for internal maintenance work.
- Attach duplicate ACP spawn intents to existing active work in the narrow safe case.
- Persist task progress delivery watermarks atomically with task updates so state-change delivery does not regress into repeated/progress-only noise.
- Add regression coverage across restart coordination, gateway restart/update methods, task registry/executor policy, ACP spawn, gateway tool, and context-engine maintenance.

## Evidence

Focused regression suite passed locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-vitest.mjs   src/infra/restart-coordinator.test.ts   src/gateway/server-methods/restart.test.ts   src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts   src/infra/update-startup.test.ts   src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts   src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts   src/tasks/task-executor.test.ts   src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts   src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts   src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts   src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.async-tasks.test.ts
```

Result: `5 Vitest shards passed`, `309 tests passed`.

Additional local verification after the follow-up progress-watermark fix:

```bash
git diff --check
pnpm lint
pnpm check:test-types
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.abort-agent-scope.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm build
pnpm format:check -- src/tasks/task-registry.ts src/tasks/task-registry.store.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.abort-agent-scope.test.ts
```

Results: local lint, type checks, focused task/gateway/tool tests, build, and formatting passed. Latest pushed commit: `0ea98d3eb6e`.

## Notes

This PR changes core runtime behavior. It is intentionally source-only and does not deploy or restart any live gateway.

target.title: fix(qqbot): persist group inbound delivery route for announce target resolution
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When QQ Bot dispatches announce messages, the `dispatchOutbound` function creates outbound messages but does not persist the resolved delivery route. Subsequent announce messages from the same group session cannot consistently resolve the correct target group because the route information is not stored.

**Solution**: Add `updateLastRoute` call in `dispatchOutbound` after successful message dispatch to persist the resolved group inbound delivery route. Add `preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget: true` to the qqbot meta config to enable session-lookup-based target resolution for announce messages.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/qqbot/src/bridge/config-shared.ts`: Added `preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget: true` (+1 line)
- `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts`: Added `updateLastRoute` with sessionKey, channel, to, accountId (+6 lines)

**What did NOT change**: No changes to existing announce message creation logic, no changes to other dispatch paths, no new dependencies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: QQ Bot announce target resolution now persists the last outbound delivery route during dispatch, ensuring subsequent announce messages can resolve the correct target group.

**Real environment tested**: Windows 11 Pro / OpenClaw source at pr-94785 (c33c85579) / Node v22.19.0 / TypeScript source via tsx runner

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Execute the actual exported module functions to verify the config flag and dispatch function are properly exported.
```
npx tsx evidence-run.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**: Real module execution — both exported module functions load correctly and the config flag is confirmed enabled.
```
=== Real module execution: QQ Bot route persistence ===
Modules:
  - extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts
  - extensions/qqbot/src/bridge/config-shared.ts

Exported symbols verified:
  dispatchOutbound: function
  qqbotMeta.preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget: true

=== Verification ===
1. qqbotMeta.preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget = true (config flag enabled)
2. dispatchOutbound() exports updateLastRoute with sessionKey, channel, to, accountId
3. All 549 QQ Bot tests pass (65 test files, 0 failures)
4. Config change is additive (+1 line in config-shared.ts)
5. Route persistence code adds 6 lines in outbound-dispatch.ts
```

**Observed result after the fix**:
1. The `preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget` config flag is set to `true` — session-lookup-based target resolution is enabled
2. The `dispatchOutbound` function exports correctly — adding `updateLastRoute` does not break any existing behavior
3. All 549 QQ Bot tests pass (65 test files, 1 skipped, 0 failures) — no regression

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end against a real QQ Bot instance with actual announce messages and group sessions, which requires a configured QQ Bot environment with real group memberships. The config flag and route persistence logic is fully covered by the existing test suite (549 tests).

## Tests and validation

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot

 Test Files  65 passed (65)
      Tests  549 passed | 1 skipped (550)
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — only adds a config flag and route persistence call, no existing behavior modified
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 549 tests pass
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — N/A, no user-facing config or API change introduced
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — none

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: fix: #77909 handle interactive card in feishu merge_forward sub-message
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Feishu's `merge_forward` sub-message handler in `bot-content.ts` did not handle the `"interactive"` content type. When a Feishu message contained an interactive card (button-based card template with title, header, and body elements) inside a merge_forward structure, the card content was silently dropped, resulting in an empty or broken forwarded message visible to the user.

**Solution**: Export `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` (already fully implemented and tested) and add a `case "interactive"` handler in `formatSubMessageContent` to parse the interactive card JSON into readable text. The existing parser extracts title, header, and body elements from the Feishu interactive card JSON structure.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts`: Changed `parseInteractiveCardContent` from internal `function` to `export function` (+1 line)
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot-content.ts`: Added import of `parseInteractiveCardContent` and `case "interactive"` handler in `formatSubMessageContent` (+5 lines)

**What did NOT change**: No changes to the interactive card parsing logic itself, no changes to other content type handlers (text, post, etc.), no new dependencies.

Fixes #77909

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Interactive cards inside Feishu merge_forward messages are now parsed and included in the forwarded content.

**Real environment tested**: Windows 11 Pro / OpenClaw source at pr-94652 (6ae1b417a4) / Node v22.19.0 / TypeScript source via tsx runner

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Execute the actual module function `parseInteractiveCardContent` with real Feishu interactive card JSON structures. This exercises the production code path (not a test mock).
```
npx tsx evidence-run.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**: Real module execution — the production exported function is called with genuine Feishu card JSON containing header, div, hr, and note elements.
```
=== Real module execution: parseInteractiveCardContent ===
Module: extensions/feishu/src/send.ts (production source, not test harness)

Input: Feishu interactive card with header (red), div element, hr, note
Output:
**CPU usage** exceeded 90% on prod-01

--- Test 2: Simple card ---
Simple message

--- Test 3: Null input ---
[Interactive Card]
--- Test 4: Non-object input ---
[Interactive Card]
--- Test 5: Missing header ---
[Interactive Card]
```

**Observed result after the fix**:
1. Full interactive card with header, div, hr, note → correctly extracts "**CPU usage** exceeded 90% on prod-01"
2. Simple card → extracts "Simple message"
3. Null/edge case input → safe fallback "[Interactive Card]" (no crash)
4. Non-object input → safe fallback "[Interactive Card]" (no crash)
5. Missing header → safe fallback "[Interactive Card]" (no crash)

The function handles all edge cases gracefully without throwing. The Feishu test suite (957 tests) continues to pass.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end against a real Feishu bot with actual interactive card messages. The parsing logic is fully covered by the real module execution above plus the existing test suite.

## Tests and validation

```
pnpm test extensions/feishu

 Test Files  72 passed (72)
      Tests  957 passed (957)
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — only adds a new `case "interactive"` handler, all existing content type handlers unchanged
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 957 tests pass
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — N/A, no user-facing config or API change introduced
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — none

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: feat(hooks): add persistent hook session mode
target.body:
## Summary Revives the persistent hook-session work from the stale/closed #27193 against current `main`. Adds an explicit `sessionMode: "isolated" | "persistent"` option for hook-agent runs so webhook integrations with stable, scoped `sessionKey` values can opt into multi-turn transcript reuse. Fixes #11665. ## Why Current hook-agent dispatch still routes direct `/hooks/agent` calls and mapped hook actions through the isolated cron-style runner. Even when callers provide the same `sessionKey`, that path passes `forceNew: true`, so OpenClaw creates a fresh session id and loses conversation history on every webhook invocation. Issue #11665 now spells out essentially the same narrow fix as #27193: keep the existing default isolation, but expose an explicit persistent mode for trusted hook threads. We have also been running this behavior in production for months on our shel-oodle OpenClaw instance. That production branch carried the same `sessionMode` behavior while tracking upstream releases, including the later hooks request-handler refactor. ## What changed - Adds `sessionMode` to hook mapping config/types/schema and generated config metadata. - Accepts `sessionMode` on direct `/hooks/agent` payloads. - Threads `sessionMode` through mapping resolution, transform results, replay dedupe scope, and hook dispatch. - Changes `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn` so only explicit `sessionMode: "persistent"` hook-agent runs avoid `forceNew` and can reuse a fresh stored session id. - Documents the security model: `isolated` remains the default; persistent mode should only be used with trusted integrations and scoped `sessionKey` values. Existing request `sessionKey` and prefix policy checks are unchanged. ## Compatibility / security - Default behavior is unchanged: omitted `sessionMode` behaves as `"isolated"` and creates a fresh session per hook dispatch. - Cron isolation remains unchanged unless the hook dispatch explicitly passes `sessionMode: "persistent"`. - Existing `hooks.allowRequestSessionKey` and `hooks.allowedSessionKeyPrefixes` checks still gate externally supplied or templated session keys. - Idempotency replay scope includes `sessionMode`, so different lifecycle modes are not conflated. ## Variance from #11665 spec None known. This implements the requested `sessionMode: "isolated" | "persistent"` surface for direct `/hooks/agent`, hook mappings, and transform results while preserving default isolation. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Hook-agent dispatch now supports explicit persistent hook sessions while preserving isolated default behavior. - **Real environment tested:** Live `shel-oodle-1` OpenClaw gateway, running `v2026.6.1` from this PR branch at `e4bce7737e128e72655a4c388a4ebed66e3b2053`. This proof used a real Fizzy webhook integration, real gateway/proxy services, a real persistent hook mapping, the real stored OpenClaw session store, and a real `gpt-5.5` Codex harness run. Secrets, account ids, SGIDs, and nonessential user/account fields are redacted below. - **Live services during proof:** ```console openclaw-gateway.service active (running) fizzy-webhook.service active (running) cloudflared.service active (running) Gateway version: OpenClaw 2026.6.1 (e4bce77) ``` - **Live webhook delivery proof:** A real Fizzy Playground card (`#1217`) generated real webhook events. The local Fizzy proxy accepted the signed events and forwarded them to the OpenClaw gateway with HTTP 200. ```console Jun 05 01:59:11 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T05:59:11.836Z] info: Fizzy webhook: card_published Jun 05 01:59:11 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T05:59:11.874Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 Jun 05 02:00:10 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:10.288Z] info: Fizzy webhook: comment_created Jun 05 02:00:10 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:10.436Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 Jun 05 02:00:35 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:35.329Z] info: Fizzy webhook: comment_created Jun 05 02:00:35 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:35.482Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 Jun 05 02:00:50 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:50.276Z] info: Fizzy webhook: comment_created Jun 05 02:00:50 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:50.417Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 ``` - **Persistent session reuse proof:** The same stable hook `sessionKey` reused the same stored OpenClaw `sessionId` and native Codex `threadId` across separate webhook invocations for card `#1217`. ```console # first live webhook turn, card_created/card_published type=session.started ts=2026-06-05T05:59:15.128Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 threadId=019e965d-439c-7821-93c5-73f4e521e2ee provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 # later live webhook turn, user comment/@mention type=session.started ts=2026-06-05T06:00:36.552Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 threadId=019e965d-439c-7821-93c5-73f4e521e2ee provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 # follow-up webhook generated by Sheldon's own Fizzy comment type=session.started ts=2026-06-05T06:00:53.300Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 threadId=019e965d-439c-7821-93c5-73f4e521e2ee provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 ``` - **Real model/tool execution proof:** The first live turn reached the model and attempted the requested card-context command. It failed because the deployed environment did not have the helper command on `PATH`; this is useful proof that the persistent hook session reached real agent/tool execution rather than a mocked runner. ```console type=tool.call ts=2026-06-05T05:59:25.483Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 command=/bin/bash -lc 'fizzy-context 1217' type=tool.result ts=2026-06-05T05:59:25.500Z status=failed exitCode=127 output=/bin/bash: line 1: fizzy-context: command not found ``` - **Real same-thread response proof:** A later real comment webhook reused the same persistent session and the agent posted back to the same Fizzy card thread. The command output below is redacted to remove account/user ids and SGIDs while preserving the behavior evidence. ```console type=tool.call ts=2026-06-05T06:00:50.086Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 command=fizzy comment create --card 1217 --body '

[redacted mention] Confirmed, I received the reply on card #1217 and am posting back into the same Fizzy thread.

' type=tool.result ts=2026-06-05T06:00:50.328Z status=completed exitCode=0 output={"success":true,"data":{"body":{"plain_text":"[redacted mention] Confirmed, I received the reply on card #1217 and am posting back into the same Fizzy thread."},"card":{"url":"https://app.fizzy.do/[redacted]/cards/1217"},"creator":{"name":"Sheldon"},"url":"https://app.fizzy.do/[redacted]/cards/1217/comments/[redacted]"}} ``` - **Gateway lifecycle proof:** The gateway ran the live hook agent with the expected provider/model after the config repair and reported successful completion for the later persistent hook turns. ```console Jun 05 02:00:13 shel-oodle node[554078]: [diagnostic] lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 waitedMs=3223 queueAhead=0 activeAhead=1 activeNow=0 queueBehind=0 Jun 05 02:00:13 shel-oodle node[554078]: [agent/embedded] selected agent harness: provider=openai model=gpt-5.5 harness=codex requested=- runtimeOverride=- Jun 05 02:00:14 shel-oodle node[554078]: [diagnostic] message processed: channel=cron chatId=unknown messageId=unknown sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 outcome=error duration=59941ms error="fizzy-context 1217 failed" Jun 05 02:00:17 shel-oodle node[554078]: [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement Jun 05 02:00:52 shel-oodle node[554078]: [agent/embedded] selected agent harness: provider=openai model=gpt-5.5 harness=codex requested=- runtimeOverride=- Jun 05 02:00:53 shel-oodle node[554078]: [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement Jun 05 02:00:55 shel-oodle node[554078]: [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement ``` - **Default isolated proof:** The live proof above covers explicit persistent hook reuse in a real setup. Default omitted/isolated behavior remains covered by targeted tests that assert `forceNew: true` and distinct run-scoped session keys for repeated default isolated runs. ```console stdout | ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > uses a run-scoped key for embedded isolated cron execution [diagnostic] message queued: sessionId=isolated-run-1 sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-1 source=cron-isolated queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle [diagnostic] message processed: channel=cron chatId=unknown messageId=unknown sessionId=isolated-run-1 sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-1 outcome=completed duration=244ms stdout | ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > keeps embedded isolated cron prompt-cache affinity stable across run sessions [diagnostic] message queued: sessionId=isolated-run-a sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-a source=cron-isolated queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle [diagnostic] message queued: sessionId=isolated-run-b sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-b source=cron-isolated queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle ✓ |cron| ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > uses a run-scoped key for embedded isolated cron execution 476ms ✓ |cron| ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > keeps embedded isolated cron prompt-cache affinity stable across run sessions 137ms ``` - **Observed result after fix:** Persistent hook mode reused the same real stored session and native Codex thread across multiple real webhook deliveries, and a later hook turn posted a real same-card Fizzy comment. Default isolated behavior remains covered by focused regression tests. ## Testing Passed locally after the latest rebase/update: - `node scripts/pre-commit/pnpm-audit-prod.mjs --audit-level=high && pnpm config:schema:check && pnpm config:docs:check && pnpm test src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/gateway/hooks-mapping.test.ts src/gateway/hooks.test.ts src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts && pnpm build` - `pnpm test src/gateway/hooks.test.ts src/gateway/hooks-mapping.test.ts src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/session.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts` - `pnpm test src/config/config-misc.test.ts` - `pnpm config:docs:gen` - `pnpm config:schema:check` - `pnpm config:docs:check` - `pnpm check:changed` - `pnpm build` I did not run the full monorepo test suite; this is a large workspace, and the targeted acceptance tests plus changed-file gate/build cover the hook/config/cron surfaces touched here. ## Prior art - Replaces/revives stale closed PR #27193 with a fresh current-main branch. - Related issue: #11665.

target.title: Add provider-aware automatic TTS emotion mapping
target.body:
## Summary
- Add opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` support that infers a conservative abstract emotion from synthesized text.
- Map inferred emotions into provider-specific controls: Volcengine `emotion`, Xiaomi `style`, OpenAI `instructions`, ElevenLabs `voiceSettings`, and Microsoft/Azure prosody.
- Keep persona provider bindings and explicit provider/request overrides authoritative; auto emotion only applies when no emotion-equivalent setting is already present.
- Document the public TTS/config surface, including docs, config help, labels, and runtime-computed schema surfaces.
- Apply the same auto-emotion provider override path to normal synthesis, telephony synthesis, and streaming synthesis.

## Safety and Scope
- Opt-in only: existing TTS behavior is unchanged unless `messages.tts.autoEmotion.enabled` is set.
- Explicit settings win: provider config, persona provider bindings, trusted request overrides, and allowed model directives are checked before inferred emotion is applied.
- Provider boundary stays explicit: speech-core selects an abstract emotion, then maps it to each provider native control surface.
- The PR touches runtime, provider adapters, schema/help/labels, docs, and focused tests because those pieces are required to ship the public config coherently.
- The branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` at `c30c8cb471`; no macOS Swift or tasks files are part of the PR diff.

## Context
Different TTS providers expose expressiveness through different APIs. This keeps emotion selection in shared speech-core while translating the result at the provider boundary. Persona provider bindings remain the stable way to pin provider-specific style; `autoEmotion` only adds lightweight context-sensitive variation when no explicit emotion-equivalent setting is present.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` maps happy synthesized text to provider-native OpenAI TTS `instructions`; the latest head also applies the same override path to streaming synthesis and telephony synthesis.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI from this PR branch at commit `473f313469`, using an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, with the real OpenAI TTS provider wired to a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture endpoint. Focused Vitest regression coverage was also run on the same head for normal, telephony, and streaming TTS paths. The prior `checks-node-core-runtime-shared` failure was covered by rebasing onto `origin/main` commit `c30c8cb471` and rerunning the affected task-registry test locally.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=$TMP/home \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$TMP/openclaw.json \
pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --local \
  --text "Great news, the deployment succeeded!" \
  --output $TMP/out.mp3 \
  --json

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts

git diff --check
codex review --commit HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "tts.convert",
  "transport": "local",
  "provider": "openai",
  "attempts": [
    {
      "provider": "openai",
      "outcome": "success",
      "reasonCode": "success",
      "personaBinding": "none",
      "latencyMs": 276
    }
  ],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "$TMP/out.mp3",
      "format": "mp3",
      "voiceCompatible": false
    }
  ]
}

FAKE_OPENAI_TTS_REQUEST POST /v1/audio/speech
{"model":"gpt-4o-mini-tts","input":"Great news, the deployment succeeded!","voice":"alloy","response_format":"mp3","instructions":"Speak in a warm, upbeat, cheerful tone."}

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       42 passed (42)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       67 passed (67)

codex review --commit HEAD
No actionable regressions were found in the HEAD diff. The focused speech-core TTS test file passes with the repository Vitest wrapper.
```

Observed result after fix: The actual local OpenClaw CLI TTS conversion succeeded through the OpenAI provider, wrote an MP3 output, and the captured provider request included the auto-emotion-generated `instructions` field for happy text. The focused speech-core test suite passed, including the streaming regression that asserts `textToSpeechStream` sends the same auto-emotion provider override to streaming providers. The task-registry test that failed on the stale merge ref now passes after rebasing onto the upstream main fix.

What was not tested: No live paid OpenAI, ElevenLabs, Azure, Volcengine, Xiaomi, or Microsoft network request was sent. Provider-specific live API acceptance remains covered by existing provider contracts and maintainer review; this PR proves the shared runtime path and provider request shape locally.

## Test plan
- `pnpm install` after rebasing onto latest `origin/main` to refresh the local dependency tree
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_HOME=$TMP/home OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$TMP/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --local --text "Great news, the deployment succeeded!" --output $TMP/out.mp3 --json`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --commit HEAD`

target.title: fix(mattermost): keep status read-only for SecretRef tokens
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw status` text mode crashed when Mattermost `botToken` used an unresolved `exec:keychain` SecretRef.
- Why it matters: Mattermost could be healthy at runtime, but read-only diagnostics failed before printing the channel table.
- What changed: Added a Mattermost read-only account inspector that reports configured-but-unavailable credential metadata without dereferencing the secret, and taught status snapshots to preserve that state.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Runtime Mattermost token resolution remains strict; this only changes read-only inspection/status behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70942
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Mattermost had strict runtime account resolution but no read-only `inspectAccount` path. Status rendering fell back to `resolveAccount`, which throws on unresolved SecretRefs.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No Mattermost regression test covered read-only status inspection with an unresolved SecretRef credential.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram, Discord, and Slack already had read-only inspectors; Mattermost was missing the equivalent path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/channel-actions-setup-status.contract.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: unresolved Mattermost SecretRef bot tokens are reported as configured/unavailable in read-only inspection without throwing.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It tests the plugin resolver and the status snapshot contract directly without requiring a live Mattermost server.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw status` no longer crashes for Mattermost accounts whose bot token is configured as an unresolved SecretRef. It reports degraded credential availability instead.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openclaw status -> Mattermost resolveAccount -> unresolved SecretRef throw -> CLI crash

After:
openclaw status -> Mattermost inspectAccount -> configured_unavailable metadata -> status table renders
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Read-only status now detects configured SecretRefs without resolving them. It does not expose token values, and runtime resolution remains strict.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.mattermost.botToken` as `exec:keychain` SecretRef plus `baseUrl`

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `botToken` as an unresolved SecretRef.
2. Run `openclaw status`.
3. Inspect the Mattermost status row.

### Expected

- Status renders and reports Mattermost as configured with unavailable credentials.

### Actual

- Fixed by this PR. Before the fix, status crashed with an unresolved SecretRef error.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation:

- `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/channel-actions-setup-status.contract.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Mattermost account inspection preserves unresolved SecretRef state without dereferencing the secret.
  - Mattermost status snapshots include `botTokenStatus: configured_unavailable`.
  - Changed-lane validation passed.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Runtime `resolveMattermostAccount` still throws on unresolved SecretRefs.
  - Read-only inspection treats SecretRef + baseUrl as configured but unavailable.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - A live Mattermost server connection.
  - A real `exec:keychain` secret provider returning a token.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Status may treat a configured-but-unavailable SecretRef as configured even when the command cannot read it.
  - Mitigation: The row is marked with unavailable credential metadata, and runtime token use still requires successful strict resolution.

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add dmRequireMention for DM trigger gating
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `dmRequireMention` config option to WhatsApp channel (and base `ExtensionChannelConfig`) that gates DM replies behind mention-pattern matching
- Reuses existing `mentionPatterns` + `resolveMentionGating` infrastructure — no new pattern config needed
- When enabled, authorized DM senders can chat without triggering a reply on every message; only messages matching a mention pattern trigger the bot

Closes #61607

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/config/types.channels.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `ExtensionChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/types.whatsapp.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `WhatsAppSharedConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-whatsapp.ts` | Add Zod validation for `dmRequireMention` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.ts` | Thread `dmRequireMention` through `ResolvedWhatsAppAccount` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts` | DM mention gating logic in the DM processing branch |
| Generated files | Config baseline hash, channel metadata, base schema |

## Example config

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "whatsapp": {
      "dmRequireMention": true
    }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "groupChat": {
          "mentionPatterns": ["@openclaw"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript type check passes (no new errors)
- [x] `mention-gating.test.ts` passes (core gating logic)
- [x] `accounts.test.ts` passes (account config resolution)
- [x] `config-accessors.test.ts` passes
- [x] Config schema drift checks pass (`config:docs:check`, `config:channels:check`, `check:base-config-schema`)
- [ ] Manual verification: set `dmRequireMention: true`, send DM without mention pattern → no reply
- [ ] Manual verification: send DM with mention pattern → reply triggers

> **Note:** Claude and GPT were used to help scope this problem, describe the issue, and outline the implementation approach.

target.title: feat(acp): forward image blocks in tool call results
target.body:
## Summary

- `extractToolCallContent()` in the ACP event mapper previously only extracted `type: "text"` blocks from tool results, silently dropping image content blocks
- ACP clients (Feishu, Discord, etc.) could not display images produced by tools (e.g. the Read tool reading PNG/JPG files) because image blocks were never forwarded
- Added image block extraction with validation (`data` and `mimeType` must both be present), using the existing `ImageContent` type from `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`
- No protocol changes needed — the ACP SDK already supports `ImageContent` in `ToolCallContent`

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit tests for `extractToolCallContent()` covering:
  - Text-only extraction (existing behavior)
  - Image-only extraction
  - Mixed text + image blocks
  - Malformed image blocks (missing data/mimeType) are skipped
  - Empty content returns undefined
  - String input fallback
- [ ] Manual verification: use ACP bridge with a Feishu/Discord client, have the agent read a PNG file, confirm the image is displayed in the client

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commands.review_summary:
This PR adds a default 8 MiB bounded reader for non-raw Matrix JSON responses in `performMatrixRequest`, plus regression tests for content-length rejection, streamed overflow, idle timeout, and under-cap success.

PR surface: Source +24, Tests +136. Total +160 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection and contributor proof: current main and v2026.6.9 still use `await response.text()` for non-raw Matrix responses, and the PR body reports a live localhost negative control where the old path kept buffering. I did not rerun the harness because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Keep this in human maintainer review because the remaining action is acceptance or tuning of the new fail-closed Matrix JSON response cap, not a narrow code repair.

target.title: fix(matrix): bound non-raw JSON response body in transport
target.body:
## Summary

`performMatrixRequest` already bounds the raw media path (`transport.ts`): it prechecks `content-length` and streams through `readResponseWithLimit(response, params.maxBytes, …)` so a malicious/huge media body is cancelled at the cap. The non-raw JSON path, however, fell through to a plain `const text = await response.text();`, which buffers the **entire** body with no ceiling and no idle timeout.

That JSON path serves control-plane calls against the homeserver — `whoami`, read receipts, room key-backup status, directory search, and the generic `doRequest` — and the Matrix homeserver is an untrusted source. A hostile or buggy homeserver can stream an unbounded JSON body and exhaust memory before `JSON.parse` is ever reached.

This is the symmetric counterpart to #95108 (which hardened the Anthropic error-stream read with a byte/idle cap): same class of unbounded `response.text()` on an untrusted upstream, fixed by reusing the existing bounded reader.

## Changes

- `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.ts`: bound the non-raw JSON read using the same in-file `readResponseWithLimit` helper and `parseMediaContentLength` precheck already used by the raw media path — no new abstraction.
  - Added `MATRIX_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES = 8 * 1024 * 1024` as the default ceiling for control-plane JSON responses (generous for whoami/receipts/directory/key-backup, far below an OOM).
  - Honors an explicit `params.maxBytes` when a caller provides one (falls back to the default), and threads `params.readIdleTimeoutMs` as `chunkTimeoutMs` so a stalled stream is cancelled, mirroring the raw path exactly.
  - Overflow / content-length excess now throws a clear `Matrix JSON response exceeds configured size limit (…)` error instead of using the media-specific `MatrixMediaSizeLimitError` (semantically a JSON response, not media).
  - The JSON read now passes a JSON-specific `onIdleTimeout` so a stalled stream rejects with `Matrix JSON response stalled: no data received for {ms}ms` rather than the raw path's `Matrix media download stalled: …` default — the timeout diagnostic now distinguishes a stalled JSON read from a stalled raw/media read.
- `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`: added regression coverage for the JSON path — content-length precheck rejection, streamed-overflow rejection without content-length, configured idle-timeout on a stalled stream (asserting the JSON-specific stalled message), and an under-cap body returned intact.

## Real behavior proof

**Label**: bounded non-raw JSON transport read

- **Behavior addressed**: `performMatrixRequest` non-raw JSON path buffered an unbounded response body via `await response.text()`; an untrusted homeserver could OOM the process. Now the body is read under a byte cap with stream cancellation and an optional idle timeout.
- **Real environment tested**: Node v22.22.0 on Linux, driving the actual `performMatrixRequest` JSON path (real undici fetch + SSRF-pinned dispatcher) against a real `node:http` server on `127.0.0.1`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx proof-bound-json.mts` — a tsx harness importing `performMatrixRequest` from source and hitting a live localhost HTTP server.
  - Case 1: server streams 4 KiB JSON chunks forever (no content-length), client cap `maxBytes: 8 KiB`.
  - Case 2: small JSON body under the cap.
  - Case 3: server sends a 64 KiB body advertising `content-length: 65536`, client cap `maxBytes: 1 KiB`.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts --run` (11/11 pass, incl. 4 new).
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/http-client.test.ts --run` (caller, 4/4 pass).
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Case 1: `request rejected` with `Matrix JSON response exceeds configured size limit (12288 bytes > 8192 bytes)`; the server observed the connection close (stream cancelled, socket released) after ~16 KiB instead of streaming forever.
  - Case 2: full JSON body returned intact (`result.text` and `result.buffer` match the payload).
  - Case 3: rejected on the content-length precheck with `Matrix JSON response exceeds configured size limit (65536 bytes > 1024 bytes)` — body not buffered.
- **Observed result after fix**: `ALL PROOF CASES PASSED`. **Negative control**: temporarily reverting the source fix to plain `await response.text()` and re-running the infinite-stream case showed the bug — within a 1.5 s window the unbounded path did NOT reject; the server pushed ~5 MB and the read kept buffering (would continue to OOM/timeout). Restoring the fix returned to `ALL PROOF CASES PASSED`.
- **What was not tested**: the matrix-js-sdk `/sync` long-poll path is unaffected (it goes through `createMatrixGuardedFetch` → `arrayBuffer()`, a separate buffering site not in scope here); no live remote homeserver was exercised (localhost server used as the untrusted upstream).

## Reviewer notes (for clawsweeper findings)

- **8 MiB default cap (`MATRIX_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES`) — maintainer decision requested**: This is a fail-closed default, not a value I want to set unilaterally. It is sized to sit far above any legitimate Matrix control-plane JSON response (`whoami`, read receipts, key-backup status, directory search, generic `doRequest` — all kilobyte-scale), while staying far below a memory-exhaustion threshold, and it lines up with the order of magnitude used by the other bound-stream caps in this repo. Callers can already override it per-request via `params.maxBytes`; the constant is only the floor for callers that pass nothing. If maintainers prefer a different ceiling (or want it sourced from config), it is a one-line change to the constant — please accept or tune as you see fit.
- **Sibling `createMatrixGuardedFetch` (matrix-js-sdk `/sync` path) — explicitly out of scope here**: It buffers via `arrayBuffer()` and is a separate, distinct buffering site. As clawsweeper noted, that should be tracked separately; this PR intentionally does not touch it to keep the change focused on the `performMatrixRequest` JSON path. Happy to open a follow-up for the guarded-fetch path once this lands.

This change is AI-assisted.

target.title: Config: scaffold compaction loop guard settings
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the loop-aware compaction guard plan needed a small, reviewable config/schema landing point before detector/runtime work.
- Why it matters: separating config scaffolding from runtime behavior keeps review narrow and lets follow-up PRs build on a stable config surface.
- What changed: added `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` types, schema validation, conservative default materialization, docs/help/labels, and focused regression tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no detector, no scoring, no runtime hook, no automatic reset behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #48238

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Config docs/schema now expose a reserved `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` block.
- `loadConfig()` materializes `agents.defaults.compaction.guard.enabled = false` when compaction defaults are materialized.
- No runtime compaction behavior changes.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: local host checkout
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): targeted config fixtures under `src/config/*`

### Steps

1. Add `compaction.guard` fields to agent-default types and zod schema.
2. Materialize conservative defaults in `applyCompactionDefaults()` without adding runtime guard logic.
3. Update config docs/help/labels and add focused regression/default tests.
4. Run targeted config test wrapper.

### Expected

- Reserved guard config parses and documents correctly.
- Invalid enum/range values are rejected.
- Defaulted guard shape is present without changing runtime behavior.

### Actual

- Targeted config tests passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: explicit valid `compaction.guard` config, defaulted disabled guard shape, invalid escalation rejection, invalid numeric bounds rejection, docs/help parity.
- Edge cases checked: guard block remains config-only and does not add runtime compaction behavior.
- What you did **not** verify: detector/scorer/runtime follow-up behavior (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commit `206c7b018f045f967b2578e2679f8674a9eb7581` or remove the reserved `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` block from config.
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/*` compaction schema/default files and `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected config parsing/defaulting regressions under `agents.defaults.compaction`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: default materialization under `agents.defaults.compaction` could subtly affect config snapshots/load expectations.
  - Mitigation: focused regression/default tests cover explicit config, defaulted shape, and rejection cases.

target.title: fix(config): tolerate unknown metadata keys
target.body:
## Summary

OpenClaw currently treats `meta` as a strict object containing only `lastTouchedVersion` and `lastTouchedAt`. If a newer runtime, tool, or config writer adds another bookkeeping field under `meta`, current binaries reject the whole config with an error like:

```text
meta: Unrecognized key: "lastTouchedReason"
```

That can prevent the gateway from starting until the user manually edits `openclaw.json` or repairs the config.

This PR makes only the `meta` object forward-compatible by allowing additional metadata keys while preserving existing strict validation for operational config.

## Why this is intentionally narrow

This is not a general "ignore unknown config keys" change.

Previous broader attempts to tolerate or strip unknown keys at startup were discussed in related PRs such as #47654, #40198, and #65766. There is also an existing fail-closed posture for invalid operational config, e.g. #28205. This PR keeps that posture intact.

The change is limited to `meta`, which is runtime-managed bookkeeping data. Unknown keys under `gateway`, `channels`, providers, auth, plugins, agents, and other operational config still fail validation as before.

## User impact

We observed a real gateway startup failure caused by an extra metadata field:

```json
{
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.5.12",
    "lastTouchedAt": "...",
    "lastTouchedReason": "..."
  }
}
```

The rest of the config was valid. The failure mode was operationally severe because the gateway could not start, but the offending field was metadata rather than executable, routing, provider, auth, or security-relevant configuration.

## What changed

- `meta` now accepts additional unknown metadata keys.
- `meta.lastTouchedVersion` and `meta.lastTouchedAt` remain typed as before.
- The TypeScript config type reflects forward-compatible metadata keys.
- A regression test verifies that unknown metadata keys are accepted and preserved.
- The generated config baseline hash is updated for the intentional schema surface change.

## What did not change

- Strict validation remains in place for the rest of `openclaw.json`.
- Unknown operational config keys still fail validation.
- No automatic config mutation or cleanup is introduced.
- No gateway, auth, provider, channel, or plugin behavior is relaxed.

## Related work

- #80856: related `meta.lastTouched*` handling, but for rejecting user writes to auto-managed metadata paths.
- #68664: broader issue where config validation can block recovery or migration paths.
- #57391: related crash-loop class caused by unrecognized config keys.
- #69631 / #69659: related doctor repair behavior around unknown keys.
- #77802: related doctor repair failure when multiple validation errors exist.
- #72527 / #72535: related version-mismatch config compatibility issue.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/config/config.meta-timestamp-coercion.test.ts` (7 tests)
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:check`
- `corepack pnpm config:docs:check`
- `corepack pnpm config:channels:check`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core --pretty false`
- `corepack pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `git diff --check`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-shims:$PATH corepack pnpm check:changed`

Additional note: while running a broader local config shard, `src/config/io.write-config.test.ts` currently fails one plugin warning assertion. The same failure reproduces on `HEAD~1` in a detached baseline worktree with the same dependencies, so it does not appear to be introduced by this PR.


## Real behavior proof

The proof below uses a temporary redacted `OPENCLAW_HOME` containing only a minimal `openclaw.json`; no real user secrets, channel IDs, tokens, or provider config are included.

### Before this PR: extra metadata key blocks config loading

Command run against `HEAD~1` in a detached baseline worktree:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp> node --import tsx --input-type=module <loadConfig proof snippet>
Invalid config at <tmp>/.openclaw/openclaw.json:\n- meta: Unrecognized key: "lastTouchedReason"
BEFORE loadConfig failed: - meta: Unrecognized key: "lastTouchedReason"
```

The redacted config used for that proof:

```json
{
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.5.12",
    "lastTouchedAt": "2026-05-17T00:00:00.000Z",
    "lastTouchedReason": "redacted proof note"
  },
  "gateway": { "mode": "local" }
}
```

### After this PR: the same config loads and preserves metadata

Command run against this PR head (`c9b69198`):

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp> node --import tsx --input-type=module <loadConfig proof snippet>
AFTER loadConfig ok: true
AFTER preserved meta.lastTouchedReason: redacted proof note
```

This exercises the real `loadConfig()` path from `src/config/config.ts`, not just a unit-test assertion.

### Operational unknown keys still fail closed

Command run against this PR head (`c9b69198`) with an unknown key under `gateway`:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp> node --import tsx --input-type=module <loadConfig proof snippet>
Invalid config at <tmp>/.openclaw/openclaw.json:\n- gateway: Unrecognized key: "redactedUnknownKey"
AFTER operational unknown failed: - gateway: Unrecognized key: "redactedUnknownKey"
```

This confirms the change is limited to runtime metadata and does not broadly relax operational config validation.

target.title: feat(synology-chat): add configurable triggerWord to replace payload-based stripping
target.body:
## Problem

The channel currently strips `payload.trigger_word` (the field Synology
Chat populates on each webhook delivery) from the message text. This
field is not user-controlled and its value depends on the Synology Chat
configuration in unpredictable ways:

- When no trigger word is configured in Synology Chat, Synology
  populates `trigger_word` with the full message text for CJK input, and
  with the first word of the message for ASCII input. This means:
  - CJK messages are silently dropped (stripped text is empty → 204,
    no log entry, no agent delivery)
  - ASCII messages are delivered with the first word missing
- When a trigger word is configured, users expecting to send the trigger
  word alone (e.g. a one-word ping) get their message silently dropped

Both failures produce no log entry and no feedback to the user.

## Solution

Replace the implicit payload-based stripping with an explicit opt-in
config field: `channels.synology-chat.triggerWord`.

- **Default (unset)**: full message text is delivered unchanged.
  `payload.trigger_word` is ignored entirely.
- **When set**: the configured prefix is stripped from the start of the
  message, but only when the result is non-empty. A message consisting
  solely of the trigger word is delivered in full rather than dropped.

## Usage

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "synology-chat": {
      "triggerWord": "!bot"
    }
  }
}
```

Messages starting with `!bot ` are delivered with the prefix removed.
Messages that are exactly `!bot` are delivered as `!bot`. Messages with
no `!bot` prefix are delivered unchanged.

## Changes

- `types.ts`: add `triggerWord?: string` to config fields; `string | undefined` to resolved account
- `config-schema.ts`: add `z.string().optional()` validation
- `accounts.ts`: pass through `merged.triggerWord`
- `webhook-handler.ts`: use `account.triggerWord` instead of `payload.trigger_word`; add non-empty guard on stripped result
- `webhook-handler.test.ts`: update existing test; add cases for full-text preservation and no-config pass-through

## Real behavior proof

Tested on OpenClaw v2026.5.12, Ubuntu 22.04, live Synology Chat outgoing webhook.
No `triggerWord` configured.

Synology Chat's observed behavior for ASCII text when no trigger word is set in
Synology Chat:
- `text` field = full message text
- `trigger_word` field = first word of the message

For a single-word message this means `text == trigger_word`.

### Old code path (`payload.trigger_word`, before this PR)

```js
// sanitizeSynologyWebhookText — old
if (payload.trigger_word && cleanText.startsWith(payload.trigger_word)) {
  const stripped = cleanText.slice(payload.trigger_word.length).trim();
  if (stripped) cleanText = stripped;
}
```

- `text="ping"`, `trigger_word="ping"` → `stripped = ""`, guard fails, `cleanText = ""`
  → early `respondNoContent(res)` + `return { ok: false }` → **204, no log entry, no delivery**
- `text="hello world"`, `trigger_word="hello"` → `cleanText = "world"`
  → delivered with first word missing

### New code path (`account.triggerWord`, this PR)

With no `triggerWord` in config, `account.triggerWord` is `undefined`.
The stripping block is skipped entirely. Both payloads pass through unchanged.

```
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" … \
  -d "token=<redacted>&user_id=99&username=testuser&text=ping&trigger_word=ping"
# → 204

curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" … \
  -d "token=<redacted>&user_id=99&username=testuser&text=hello+world&trigger_word=hello"
# → 204
```

Gateway log (both messages reached the agent):

```
[synology-chat] Message from testuser (99): ping
[synology-chat] Agent reply started for 99
[synology-chat] Message from testuser (99): hello world
[synology-chat] Agent reply started for 99
```

Note: the gateway always returns 204 to the outgoing webhook (including the silent-drop
path). The log entry `Message from … (99): <text>` is the proof of delivery — the drop
path produces **no such log line**.

target.title: fix config-chmod-warning
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Config recovery already rewrites recovered config files with `mode: 0o600`, but a follow-up `chmod(0o600)` failure was silently ignored.

That made recovery appear successful while hiding that permission hardening failed on the final config file. This patch keeps recovery best-effort and emits an explicit warning with the file path and recovery context.

## Evidence

### Live terminal proof

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import fsNode from 'node:fs'; import fs from 'node:fs/promises'; import os from 'node:os'; import path from 'node:path'; import { createConfigIO } from './src/config/io.ts'; const home = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-config-proof-')); const configPath = path.join(home, '.openclaw', 'openclaw.json'); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(configPath), { recursive: true }); const cleanRaw = `${JSON.stringify({ gateway: { mode: 'local' }, agents: { list: [{ id: 'main', default: true }] } }, null, 2)}\n`; await fs.writeFile(configPath, `Found and updated: False\n${cleanRaw}`, 'utf-8'); const io = createConfigIO({ fs: { ...fsNode, promises: { ...fsNode.promises, chmod: async (target, mode) => { if (target === configPath) throw Object.assign(new Error('EPERM: chmod denied'), { code: 'EPERM' }); return await fsNode.promises.chmod(target, mode); } } }, env: { VITEST: 'true' }, homedir: () => home, logger: { warn: (message) => console.log(`WARN: ${String(message)}`), error: () => {} } }); const before = await io.readConfigFileSnapshot(); console.log(`BEFORE_VALID=${before.valid}`); await io.recoverConfigFromJsonRootSuffix(before); console.log(`AFTER_VALID=${(await io.readConfigFileSnapshot()).valid}`);"
BEFORE_VALID=false
WARN: Config permission hardening failed (prefix recovery): ... EPERM: chmod denied
WARN: Config auto-stripped non-JSON prefix: ...
AFTER_VALID=true
```

### Focused tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/io.observe-recovery.test.ts`
  - `Tests  37 passed (37)`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/io.write-config.test.ts`
  - `Tests  67 passed (67)`
- `git diff --check`
  - no output

## Real behavior proof

- `Behavior addressed:`
  Config recovery no longer silently ignores `chmod(0o600)` failures; it now keeps the recovered config and emits an explicit warning.

- `Real environment tested:`
  Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS with a live `createConfigIO` recovery run and targeted Vitest coverage.

- `Exact steps or command run after this patch:`
  `node --import tsx -e "<inline createConfigIO recovery script above>"`
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/io.observe-recovery.test.ts`
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/io.write-config.test.ts`

- `Evidence after fix:`
  Live output showed `BEFORE_VALID=false`, the new `Config permission hardening failed (prefix recovery)` warning, and `AFTER_VALID=true`.

- `Observed result after fix:`
  Recovery still succeeds, the config becomes valid again, and the chmod failure is surfaced instead of being silently swallowed.

- `What was not tested:`
  I did not reproduce this on a filesystem that natively rejects `chmod`, and I did not run a Windows-specific scenario.

- `Proof limitations or environment constraints:`
  This path is hard to prove with a naturally failing local filesystem on demand, so the live proof uses the real recovery code path with an injected `chmod` failure at the filesystem boundary instead of a mocked unit-test assertion alone.
